# **Architectural Blueprint for an In-Browser Educational Large Language Model: The Othello-GPT Paradigm**

## **Introduction to the Browser-Based AI Frontier**

The democratization of machine learning training architectures has reached a critical inflection point, enabled by the advent of WebGPU and advanced JavaScript compilation targets. The ambition to create a self-contained, browser-based educational platform that guides a user through the complete lifecycle of a modern Large Language Model (LLM)—from pre-training to Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR)—requires a highly optimized, domain-specific approach. The challenge lies in selecting a "toy problem" that is computationally feasible within the constraints of consumer hardware VRAM, yet structurally complex enough to exhibit the hallmark behaviors of frontier models like DeepSeek-R1, including emergent world representations and logical reasoning1.  
This report proposes a comprehensive architectural blueprint for an educational platform, provisionally aligned with the "Vibe Engine" ecosystem, leveraging modern frontend frameworks like Svelte and TypeScript3. The selected problem domain is the training of an autoregressive Transformer on the game of Othello (Othello-GPT). By modeling a game board as a sequence of tokens, the system perfectly mirrors the linguistic sequence modeling of text-based LLMs while remaining bounded to a vocabulary size of 61 and a context window of 59 tokens5. At approximately 25 million parameters, this architecture is lightweight enough to be trained iteratively in the browser, yet robust enough to benefit from modern pipeline techniques, including Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) and Mechanistic Interpretability probing1.

## **The Optimal Toy Problem: Evaluating the Candidates**

When evaluating potential toy problems to simulate LLM training, several candidates arise, including basic arithmetic, Sokoban puzzle planning, chess, and Othello. The selection must satisfy strict pedagogical and technical criteria: it must be self-contained, feature a finite state space, possess a clear verification mechanism for reinforcement learning, and be trainable within the strict limits of WebGPU memory allocations.

| Candidate Domain | Vocabulary Size | Context Window | Complexity | Verdict for Browser Training |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Chess** | High (Move combinations) | Variable, often \> 100 | Extreme | **Suboptimal.** Massive game tree; requires high parameter counts to master8. Variable game lengths complicate fixed-tensor memory allocation in JS. |
| **Sokoban** | Medium (Grid tokens) | Highly Variable | NP-Complete | **Suboptimal.** Highly susceptible to dead-ends; requires hierarchical reinforcement learning and complex sub-goal generation9. Sparse rewards make RLVR convergence too slow for an interactive course. |
| **Basic Math** | Very Small (0-9, Operators) | Small | Low | **Suboptimal.** While easy to verify, it lacks the spatial and geometric complexity required to demonstrate emergent "world models" and deeper mechanistic interpretability. |
| **Othello** | 61 (Board positions \+ Pass) | Fixed Maximum (60) | Moderate | **Optimal.** Fixed context window, bounded vocabulary, well-defined rules for JS-based RLVR engines, and proven to develop linear emergent world representations5. |

### **Structural Equivalencies to Linguistic LLMs**

Othello provides the optimal mathematical and structural properties for an educational LLM environment. The game consists of an 8x8 grid, with 4 center squares pre-filled (D3, D4, E3, E4), leaving 60 possible moves5.  
Othello-GPT translates the geometric game into a raw vocabulary problem. The board coordinates (e.g., A1, C4) are mapped to integers, creating a vocabulary size of 61 (60 board positions plus a special masking/pass token)5. The model is not explicitly programmed with the rules of Othello, the concept of a 2D grid, or the existence of black and white pieces. Instead, it is fed flat sequences of random, legal moves, forcing it to predict the next legal token via standard cross-entropy loss6.  
This paradigm is invaluable for an educational platform because it serves as a perfect microcosm of language modeling. Just as a text-based LLM learns the syntax of English and factual world knowledge simply by predicting the next word, Othello-GPT learns the underlying rules of the game and internalizes a linear representation of the 2D board state simply by predicting the next coordinate2. The translation of spatial dynamics into sequential token prediction is what enables the educational leap from a board game to natural language processing.

![][image1]

### **Parameter Feasibility and Memory Bounds**

Prior research has demonstrated that a Transformer with 8 layers, 8 attention heads, and a hidden dimension (![][image2]) of 512 requires roughly 25 million parameters to achieve a near-zero error rate in predicting legal Othello moves11. While the overarching platform goals allow for scaling up to 500 million parameters, maintaining the model around the 25 to 50 million parameter mark is strategically optimal for in-browser training.  
Modern WebGPU implementations can efficiently handle matrix multiplications for models scaling up to 1.5 billion parameters for *inference*13, but *training* requires retaining massive activation gradients, Adam optimizer states (first and second moments), and the computational graphs in VRAM. A 25M parameter model requires significantly less memory overhead, ensuring that training batches can be processed rapidly enough to provide the real-time visual feedback necessary for an interactive educational tool without triggering browser-level Out-of-Memory (OOM) GPU context crashes14.

## **Client-Side Compute Infrastructure: Building the "Vibe Engine"**

To execute the entire training pipeline in the browser, the platform must circumvent the limitations of standard JavaScript execution. The conventional approach for in-browser AI relies on ONNX Runtime and Transformers.js, which compile pre-trained models into WebAssembly and WebGPU for fast client-side inference16. However, Transformers.js is strictly an inference library; it cannot perform automatic differentiation, backpropagation, or parameter updates18.  
To build an educational tool that trains the model, the architecture must leverage a complete tensor computation and autograd library optimized for WebGPU.

### **Backend Compute Alternatives**

Three primary technological pathways exist for in-browser automatic differentiation and tensor math:

| Framework | Architecture Paradigm | WebGPU Support | Autograd Capabilities | Pedagogical Suitability |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Transformers.js** | ONNX Runtime Wrapper | Excellent (v4) | None (Inference Only) | **Low.** Cannot train models17. |
| **Webgpu-torch / Piston** | PyTorch API Clone | Moderate (Custom WGSL) | Yes (In-development) | **Moderate.** Excellent API familiarity, but custom WGSL kernels are still lacking optimization for full multi-layer distributed attention19. |
| **Jax-js** | JAX API Clone | Excellent (JIT Compiled) | Yes (Robust) | **High.** Compiles high-level ops to WGSL via a lightweight compiler; capable of 7000 GFLOP/s on Apple Silicon15. |

For the Vibe Engine, jax-js serves as the optimal backend engine. Recent open-source experiments, such as quectoGPT, have demonstrated that full GPT training loops—including byte-pair tokenization, batched forward/backward passes, and optax AdamW optimization—can be run flawlessly in the browser using jax-js21.

### **Client-Side Memory Management and Concurrency**

A critical engineering challenge in browser-based training is memory management. JavaScript lacks standard object destructors for heavy memory objects, making garbage collection of large tensor arrays a bottleneck that frequently leads to WebGL/WebGPU context crashes14. The jax-js library addresses this via reference-counted ownership. Tensors must be manually managed using .ref (incrementing the reference count) and .dispose() (decrementing it and freeing GPU memory)15.  
The educational platform will abstract this memory management into the backend of the Vibe Engine. Integrating this with a Svelte frontend requires delegating the jax-js training loop to a dedicated Web Worker. This ensures that the heavy synchronous WGSL compilation and asynchronous GPU buffer mapping do not block the main UI thread21. The Web Worker will periodically post message payloads containing the current loss metrics, epoch progress, and sampled logits back to the Svelte components for live visualization3.

## **Pipeline Phase 1: Pre-Training Architecture and Mechanics**

The educational journey begins with the foundational pre-training phase. The objective is to teach the autoregressive Transformer the fundamental physics and rules of the Othello environment via next-token prediction.

### **Architectural Modernization (The "DeepSeek" Enhancements)**

While the original Othello-GPT utilized a standard GPT-2 style architecture12, an educational tool designed to mirror modern models (e.g., Llama 3, Qwen, DeepSeek) must implement state-of-the-art structural modifications. The course will guide users through constructing the network step-by-step, explaining the mathematical superiority of modern techniques.

> 1. **Rotary Position Embeddings (RoPE):** Instead of standard learned absolute position embeddings, the model will implement RoPE. RoPE encodes positional information by rotating the key and query representations in the complex plane. This teaches the user how modern models handle relative distances between tokens, a concept that scales dynamically compared to absolute embeddings.  
> 2. **SwiGLU Activation Functions:** Standard ReLU or GELU activations5 will be replaced by SwiGLU (Swish-Gated Linear Unit). This introduces the concept of gating mechanisms within the Feed-Forward Network (FFN), which empirically accelerates convergence and is utilized in nearly all frontier open-source models.  
> 3. **Mixture of Experts (MoE) Routing (Optional Advanced Module):** To mimic models like DeepSeek-V3 or Mixtral, the platform will feature an interactive toggle to convert the dense FFN layers into a sparse MoE architecture. Users will visualize how a router network assigns different Othello board tokens to specific "expert" FFNs, significantly reducing active parameters during the forward pass while maintaining model capacity.

### **The Synthetic Dataset and Batched Inference**

The pre-training corpus utilizes a "Synthetic" dataset consisting of 20 million randomly generated Othello games11. Because the moves are uniformly sampled from a legal game tree, the dataset contains no strategic intent; it purely represents the physics and rules of the board11.  
To ensure the browser does not freeze during data loading, the system will utilize IndexedDB to cache pre-tokenized binary arrays of the game transcripts. The training loop, facilitated by jax-js, will implement window-based sampling—extracting random blockSize-length windows from the flat token stream without the need for complex padding or masking21. A batch size of 8 or 16 will be processed, calculating the cross-entropy loss over the 61-token vocabulary.  
As the loss curve drops in real-time on the Svelte dashboard, the user will see the model transition from predicting impossible moves to predicting strictly legal moves with a near-zero error rate6.

## **Pipeline Phase 2: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT)**

Once the model learns the rules of Othello, the curriculum advances to Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT). Pre-training simply teaches the model to play randomly; SFT teaches the model to play *well*.

### **The SFT Curriculum and Distribution Shift**

The educational platform will seamlessly swap the dataset from the "Synthetic" random transcripts to a "Championship" dataset. This dataset comprises approximately 140,000 games played by expert human players, capturing high-level strategic intent11.  
In this module, the user will observe a phenomenon central to modern LLM development: distribution shift. As the model fine-tunes on the Championship data using a linear learning rate decay21, its loss on the random dataset will increase, but its ability to predict high-quality, strategic moves will sharpen. The probability distributions of the output logits will transition from being relatively uniform across all legal moves to highly spiked over specific strategic placements (e.g., corner capturing).  
The UI will feature an interactive Othello board where the user can play against the model in real-time. Prior to SFT, the model will make legal but chaotic moves, easily defeated by the user. Following SFT, the user will physically experience the model executing tactical maneuvers and demonstrating long-term planning, cementing the purpose of the SFT phase.

![][image3]

## **Pipeline Phase 3: Alignment via GRPO and RLVR**

The most advanced and critical chapter of the educational platform involves aligning the model using Reinforcement Learning. Traditional alignment methodologies, such as Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO), require loading several distinct models into memory: the Actor (the model being trained), the Reference Model, a Reward Model, and a Value (Critic) Model1. Implementing PPO in the browser is largely unfeasible due to the severe VRAM constraints of running these simultaneous instances.  
To solve this, the platform will teach Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), the exact algorithmic breakthrough pioneered in the DeepSeek-Math and DeepSeek-R1 architectures25.

### **The Mathematics of GRPO**

GRPO eliminates the need for the highly memory-intensive Critic/Value network. Instead of estimating a baseline absolute value for an action using a separate neural network, GRPO relies on group-based statistical relative comparisons1.  
For a given prompt (a partial Othello game state, ![][image4]), the old policy generates a group of ![][image5] different possible completions (move sequences). Each completion is scored by a reward function to yield a raw reward ![][image6]. GRPO then calculates the advantage ![][image7] of each sequence by normalizing it against the group's mean and standard deviation:  
![][image8]  
This relative comparison means the model is updated based on whether an output is better or worse than its own average attempts within that specific batch, drastically improving sample efficiency while halving the memory requirement1.  
The core GRPO objective function introduces a surrogate clipping mechanism to prevent destructive, overly large policy updates. It maximizes the probability ratio of the new policy ![][image9] over the old policy ![][image10], clipped by a parameter ![][image11] (typically 0.2)28. Furthermore, to maintain stability and prevent the model from deviating entirely into ungrammatical or illegal outputs, a Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence penalty is integrated into the loss function, anchoring the training policy to the original frozen SFT reference policy27.  
![][image12]

### **Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR)**

In modern reasoning models, creating the reward signal (![][image6]) is the primary bottleneck. Traditional Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) requires human annotators or larger LLMs acting as judges, which introduces latency, bias, and impossible scaling requirements for a browser environment. Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) bypasses this by utilizing deterministic, programmatic environments31.  
Othello is the quintessential environment for RLVR. The educational platform will feature a deterministic JavaScript rule-engine that validates the model's generated sequence33. The user will interactively code the reward function directly in the browser editor.  
A standard RLVR configuration module will allow the user to tweak the following heuristic reward parameters:

| Reward Category | Verification Mechanism | Default Reward (ri​) | Pedagogical Purpose |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Format Validation** | Regex/Integer bounds check | ![][image13] | Teaches the model to output valid token integers (1-60) without hallucinating out-of-bound tokens31. |
| **Legality Verification** | JavaScript Othello rule-engine | ![][image14] | Ensures the model only proposes moves that strictly adhere to game physics (flanking opponent pieces)34. |
| **Strategic Dominance** | Positional matrix evaluation | ![][image15] / ![][image16] | Rewards capturing corner squares (immutable pieces); penalizes placing pieces adjacent to corners (high risk)36. |
| **Outcome Verification** | End-state win/loss calculation | ![][image17] | Final sparse reward granted if the generated sequence results in winning the game against a baseline heuristic bot10. |

By combining GRPO's memory efficiency with RLVR's deterministic feedback, the user executes a state-of-the-art alignment loop on their local machine. They will witness the model autonomously adapt its strategy based on the programmed environmental constraints, mirroring the exact methodology used to train large-scale mathematical and coding LLMs25.

![][image18]

## **Pipeline Phase 4: Mechanistic Interpretability**

The final pedagogical stage validates the success of the pre-training and alignment phases by literally "looking inside" the neural network. Mechanistic interpretability is the process of reverse-engineering the computational graphs of AI models to understand how they arrive at their outputs37.  
In the original Othello-GPT research, investigators discovered that despite being trained entirely on flat sequences of integers, the model developed a distinct, linear emergent world representation of the 2D board state7. The model did not categorize squares by their absolute color ("Black" vs. "White"), but by their relative ownership status ("Mine", "Theirs", "Empty"), demonstrating high-level conceptual abstraction7.

### **Linear Probes and Activation Patching**

The Vibe Engine will provide an interactive interpretability dashboard. Users will extract the intermediate activations from the model's residual stream during the forward pass. By targeting specific layers—such as layer 5 or 6, where researchers have noted spatial processing and tile stability computations culminate38—the user can train a "linear probe." A linear probe is a simple, single-layer linear classifier trained to predict the physical board state strictly from the internal high-dimensional vectors of the LLM7.  
By analyzing the accuracy of the probe, users will witness the mathematical proof of the model's internal map of the game board. To prove causality rather than just correlation, the platform will guide users through an advanced "Activation Patching" exercise37.  
In this interactive module, the user will manually intervene in the model's hidden layers. Using the vector direction established by the linear probe, they will artificially alter the activation values to effectively "flip" the model's belief about a specific square from "Theirs" to "Mine"7. Upon running the remainder of the forward pass after this manual intervention, the user will observe the model output a completely different next token, proving that the model's final prediction is causally driven by its internal world representation2.  
This creates an unparalleled cognitive breakthrough for a student. It transforms the LLM from an opaque statistical black box relying on "surface statistics" into a highly structured, manipulable algorithmic engine, closing the educational loop41.

## **Strategic Synthesis and Platform Deployment**

The pursuit of teaching modern Large Language Model architectures within a completely client-side browser environment necessitates an exact balance of domain selection, compute optimization, and curriculum design.  
By anchoring the educational platform to the Othello-GPT paradigm, the system resolves the tension between computational constraints and architectural complexity. A 25-million parameter autoregressive sequence model is small enough to train locally via WebGPU and jax-js memory management, yet it acts as a perfect functional equivalent to massive text-based models.  
Through this proposed "Vibe Engine" platform, the user executes the exact lifecycle of a frontier AI system. They pre-train on synthetic data to construct a foundational world model, fine-tune on expert data to shift probability distributions toward strategic reasoning, and apply GRPO and Verifiable Rewards to align the agent without exhausting consumer GPU resources. The culmination of this pipeline via mechanistic interpretability ensures that the fundamental mechanics of the modern LLM are not merely observed, but mathematically proven and deeply understood directly within the browser.
